perturbation.original_text: 14. This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of Japan.
perturbation.changed_text:
6. The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information shall be disclosed to only those people within its respective organizations or its agents, consultants, representatives or advisors who have a need to know the information 2 <PAGE> and who are obligated under terms no less restrictive than those imposed by this Agreement on the Receiving Party.

14. This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of Japan.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the governing law clause to be buried within the confidentiality clause makes it less prominent and harder to find, potentially leading to disputes over which jurisdiction's laws apply. This creates ambiguity and reduces enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 emphasizes the importance of a clear and express choice-of-law provision. Burying this clause within a different section obscures its intent and prominence, potentially leading a court to question its validity or enforceability, especially if the placement suggests it's merely a sub-clause of the confidentiality provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. [Last reviewed in August of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS multi-jurisdictional law conflicts of law THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure legal practice/ethics wex articles wex definitions civil procedure constitutional law criminal law and procedure legal education and practice Resources menu of sources Federal Material U.S. Constitution Article VI - Supremacy Clause ; CRS Annotated text Article IV, Section 1 - Full Faith and Credit Clause ; CRS Annotated text Federal Statutes 28 U.S.C. §§1738-1739 - State Proceedings 18 U.S.C. §§ 848 , 896 , 927 , 2345 47 U.S.C. § 741- Satellite and Communications Law 19 U.S.C. § 2504 - Trade Agreements 45 U.S.C. § 1213 - Railroads Federal Judicial Decisions U.S. Supreme Court: Recent Conflicts Decisions liibulletin Oral Argument Previews U.S. Circuit Courts of Appeals: Recent Conflicts Decisions State Material State Judicial Decisions N.Y. Court of Appeals: Recent Conflicts Decisions Commentary from liibulletin-ny Appellate Decisions from Other States International Material Conventions and Treaties Convention on the Law Applicable to Contract Obligations (1980) Other References Useful Offnet (or Subscription - $) Sources Good Starting Point in Print: Eugene F. Scoles and Peter Hay, Hornbook on Conflict of Laws , West Group (2004) other topics Category: International, Transnational, and Comparative Law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that relocating the governing law clause makes it less prominent and harder to find, potentially leading to disputes. The LLM Law Explanation bolsters this by stating the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 emphasizes the importance of a clear and express choice-of-law provision and that burying this clause within a different section obscures its intent. The perturbation type, 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction' applies because the clause was moved to a less prominent and more ambiguous location, affecting its clarity and enforceability, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides some context by stating "conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute." Making the clause harder to find or less prominent increases the chance of conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 15. The Receiving Party acknowledges that remedies of damages may be inadequate to protect against breach of this Agreement and the Receiving Party agrees in 3 <PAGE> advance to the granting of injunctive or other equitable relief to the Disclosing Party in addition to any other remedy which may be available to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text:
9. Disclosure of any information under this Agreement, or otherwise, shall not be construed as granting, directly or by implication, any license under or interest of any kind in any patent, patent application, copyright or other intellectual property rights.

15. The Receiving Party acknowledges that remedies of damages may be inadequate to protect against breach of this Agreement and the Receiving Party agrees in 3 <PAGE> advance to the granting of injunctive or other equitable relief to the Disclosing Party in addition to any other remedy which may be available to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the equitable relief clause into the intellectual property section makes it seem like equitable relief is only available for IP-related breaches, limiting its application to other breaches of the agreement, and significantly reducing its enforceability in non-IP contexts.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-305 states that remedies should be liberally administered to put the aggrieved party in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed. By moving the equitable relief clause to the intellectual property section, you imply that this clause only applies to those violations, contradicting the purpose to apply to the entire agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-305. Remedies to be Liberally Administered. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-305. Remedies to be Liberally Administered. (a) The remedies provided by [the Uniform Commercial Code] must be liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed but neither consequential or special damages nor penal damages may be had except as specifically provided in [the Uniform Commercial Code] or by other rule of law. (b) Any right or obligation declared by [the Uniform Commercial Code] is enforceable by action unless the provision declaring it specifies a different and limited effect. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change re-locates a clause about equitable relief, which originally applied to the entire agreement, into a section dealing with intellectual property. This structural change implies the clause only applies to IP breaches, thereby limiting the scope of remedies available for other breaches of the agreement. UCC § 1-305(a) states that remedies should be liberally administered to put the aggrieved party in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed. By limiting the equitable relief clause's applicability, the changed text contradicts the UCC's intent to provide broad and adequate remedies, indicating a clear non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 11. The parties hereto shall not be obligated to compensate each other for the disclosure and/or use pursuant to the terms of this Agreement of any information exchanged in connection with this Agreement or the discussions between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text:
1. (a) All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Confidential Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form and marked "Confidential," with the name of the Disclosing Party and the date of disclosure. If the Confidential Information is initially disclosed orally, it shall be reduced to written form by the Disclosing Party (including the date of the oral disclosure and name of the Disclosing Party) and presented or mailed to the Receiving Party within fifteen (15) days of the first oral disclosure.

11. The parties hereto shall not be obligated to compensate each other for the disclosure and/or use pursuant to the terms of this Agreement of any information exchanged in connection with this Agreement or the discussions between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the "no compensation" clause into the section defining confidential information makes it appear as though it only applies to the exchange of confidential information and does not extend to the use of that information. This may give the impression that information used outside the bounds of the agreement is subject to compensation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 202 emphasizes interpreting a contract as a whole. Moving the 'no compensation' clause to be directly after the 'Confidential Information' definition would create the impression that the clause is intended to only apply to confidential information, not the entire agreement, which would contradict the intent of the full agreement.

perturbation.justification: The relocation of the 'no compensation' clause, as described in the Perturbation Explanation, introduces a structural flaw that could reasonably lead to the interpretation that it solely applies to confidential information. This interpretation contradicts the original agreement's intent, potentially leading to disputes over compensation for information used outside the bounds of confidential information. While the Scraped Law Snippets are not directly applicable, the Law Explanation referencing Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 202 supports the idea that contracts should be interpreted as a whole, which this change undermines. This aligns with the definition of a 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction,' where the placement of a clause affects clarity and enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 16. The Disclosing Party does not make any representation or warranty, except as may be specifically provided in writing, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information, or as to its utility or suitability for any purpose of the Receiving Party and the Disclosing Party expressly disclaims any right of the Receiving Party to rely thereon, or any liability to the Receiving Party resulting from the use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text:
8. If any official approval is required by a government authority or disclose the Confidential Information hereunder, such disclosure is subject to that approval. Both parties shall comply in all respects with applicable laws, regulations and court orders, including but not limited to laws and regulations on export control, in both parties' countries and other applicable countries.

16. The Disclosing Party does not make any representation or warranty, except as may be specifically provided in writing, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information, or as to its utility or suitability for any purpose of the Receiving Party and the Disclosing Party expressly disclaims any right of the Receiving Party to rely thereon, or any liability to the Receiving Party resulting from the use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By moving the disclaimer of warranty clause to be included in the 'Compliance' Section, the clause now appears to only related to disclosures done in compliance with 'applicable laws, regulations, and court orders'. This could cause a court to rule that the disclaimer does not apply to other disclosures done in violation of said laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under UCC § 2-316, disclaimers of warranty must be conspicuous and unambiguous. Embedding it within the legal compliance section obscures its broader application and intent to disclaim warranties beyond legally compelled disclosures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by potentially limiting the scope of the warranty disclaimer. UCC § 2-316 requires disclaimers to be conspicuous. By moving the disclaimer into the compliance section, it creates ambiguity as to whether it applies only to disclosures made under legal compulsion, thus weakening its overall effect and contradicting the requirement for a clear and unambiguous disclaimer, as the LLM Law Explanation states. This is because a court may rule that the disclaimer does not apply to disclosures done in violation of said laws.

perturbation.original_text: 8. If any official approval is required by a government authority or disclose the Confidential Information hereunder, such disclosure is subject to that approval. Both parties shall comply in all respects with applicable laws, regulations and court orders, including but not limited to laws and regulations on export control, in both parties' countries and other applicable countries.
perturbation.changed_text:
3. Either party hereto shall have the right, at any time, to terminate in writing and discussions and exchange of information in connection with the exploration of the possibilities of a business relationship between the parties without any further obligations or liabilities to the other party, other than the obligations of confidentiality hereunder, or any right or obligation relating to the Confidential Information hereunder.

8. If any official approval is required by a government authority or disclose the Confidential Information hereunder, such disclosure is subject to that approval. Both parties shall comply in all respects with applicable laws, regulations and court orders, including but not limited to laws and regulations on export control, in both parties' countries and other applicable countries.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the compliance clause next to the termination clause gives the impression that compliance with laws is only required for agreements that have not been terminated. This could cause ambiguity regarding the continued obligations of the parties with respect to applicable laws after the termination of the agreement, potentially leading to violations of export control and other laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: EAR 15 CFR § 732.7 requires continued compliance with export control laws even after a transaction is completed or an agreement is terminated. Positioning the general compliance clause next to the termination clause may imply that such compliance obligations end upon termination, contradicting export control regulations and creating a legal risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a structural flaw by potentially leading to the interpretation that compliance with laws is only required for agreements that have not been terminated. This contradicts the principle that certain legal obligations, such as those related to export control (as suggested by the Law Explanation mentioning EAR 15 CFR § 732.7), continue even after the termination of an agreement. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the ambiguity introduced by the clause's new placement. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is incomplete, the LLM Law explanation sufficiently states that compliance is needed after termination, which is contradicted by the changed agreement text.
